root.title: Cúram 8.1.2 Outcome Management Guide
root.summary: Front matter and main content for the Cúram 8.1.2 Outcome Management Guide. Introduces outcome management as a client-centric, collaborative approach focused on desired client results, then explains how to configure and use assessments and outcome plans to identify needs, build goals/objectives/activities, assign MDT teams, manage agreements and visitation plans, and monitor progress through reassessments, reviews, reporting, notes, attachments, correspondence, related cases, and productivity views. Also covers completing actions/services/goals/objectives and closing outcome plans. The section includes the note, edition, contents, and legal notices/privacy/trademark pages. Useful if you need either the conceptual model or operational workflow for outcome management, plus the publication’s usage/legal terms.

children.title: Note
children.summary: This note directs readers to review the Notices section on page 47 before using the product or this information. It also shows the copyright notice for Merative US L.P. covering 2012 and 2024.

children.title: Edition
Edition — pages 5-14
children.summary: Introductory/front-matter section for the Cúram Outcome Management Guide. States the edition applies to Cúram 8.1, 8.1.1, and 8.1.2, with copyright/trademark notices and table of contents. Begins Chapter 1, explaining Outcome Management as a client-centric, collaborative approach focused on identifying root causes, planning activities, monitoring progress, and achieving positive outcomes. Introduces configuration of assessments and outcome plans, including assessment types, roles, factors, categories/classifications, and planned outcome plan goals.

children.title: Edition — pages 15-24
children.summary: This section introduces Outcome Management configuration and use: outcome plan types, goals, objectives, factors, evidence, multidisciplinary teams, visits, printable report content, assessments, activities, services, referrals, actions, recommendations, guidance text, assessment approval, and collaboration. It explains how administrators define reusable elements across plan types, how caseworkers add and manage them on client plans, and how assessment results are scored, displayed, prioritized, and approved. It also covers sharing information with multidisciplinary teams via the portal and configuring visit and document content options.

children.title: Edition — pages 25-34
children.summary: Covers outcome plan edition features: multidisciplinary team sharing and collaboration (shared notes, attachments, meetings, discussion boards, meeting minutes), setting goals and objectives, and planning activities in the workspace/recommendations panes. Also describes action settings such as cost, required participation, ownership and responsibility. Includes client agreements (creation, approval, issuing, signing), visitation plans and logs, and introduces monitoring progress with analysis view and reassessment of client needs over time.

children.title: Edition — pages 35-44
children.summary: This section covers post-assessment and outcome plan management in Outcome Management. It explains reassessment options, comparing assessment results, viewing history, summaries, approval requests, and generating assessment reports. It also describes closing assessments, status values, factor reprioritization and lapsing, and review workflows for outcome plans. Additional tools include productivity summaries, “my outcome plans,” file attachments, reports, correspondence, notes, related cases, and tracking progress/costs. Useful if you need workflows, statuses, reports, or caseworker navigation features around assessments and plans.

children.title: Edition — pages 45-46
children.summary: Explains how to complete actions, services, goals, and objectives in an outcome plan. It says actions/services are marked complete when they end, with outcomes recorded as successful or not successful; failures require a reason, and actions may also record actual cost if configured. Goals/objectives are completed to indicate whether outcomes were attained, with reasons required if not attained. It recommends closing the outcome plan only after all goals and objectives have an outcome, changing status from Open to Closed.

Intro/front matter for the Cúram Outcome Management Guide (applies to Cúram 8.1, 8.1.1, 8.1.2) plus the full chapter on outcome management. Covers the client-centric, collaborative model; configuring assessments and outcome plans; creating plans, goals, objectives, activities, MDT collaboration, agreements, and visitation plans; monitoring progress via reassessment, reviews, status changes, reports, notes, attachments, correspondence, related cases, and productivity views; and completing actions/services/goals/objectives and closing plans. Good for finding both conceptual overviews and operational workflow details.

children.title: Notices
Privacy policy
children.summary: This section contains legal notices: fictitious-name disclaimer for examples, copyright/license terms for sample source code, warranty/liability disclaimer, a link to the Merative privacy policy, trademark notices for Merative and third-party brands, and the copyright notice © Merative US L.P. 2012, 2024.

children.title: Trademarks
children.summary: States that sample names in the document are fictitious, and that sample source programs may be copied, modified, and distributed under the Merative Client Agreement for API-conforming applications, but are provided “AS IS” without warranty. Includes a privacy policy link and a trademarks notice listing Merative, IBM, Adobe, Oracle/Java, Linux, Microsoft/Windows, and UNIX as protected marks, plus a copyright notice for Merative US L.P. (2012, 2024).

This notice page states Merative’s publication permissions and limits: personal noncommercial copying is allowed with notices preserved, and enterprise use is allowed only internally, but no derivative works or external distribution without consent. It disclaims warranties, notes export-law compliance, and says Merative may withdraw permissions. It also states no patent license is granted, non-Merative product info is unverified, external links are not endorsements, and the publication is provided “as-is.” Includes copyright © Merative US L.P. 2012, 2024.

children.title: Contents
children.summary: Table of contents for the guide. Lists the front matter (Note, Edition) and the main chapter “Outcome Management Guide,” with subsections on understanding outcome management, configuring assessments and outcome plans, building plans for clients, and monitoring client progress. Also includes notices, privacy policy, trademarks, and copyright. Useful as a navigation page rather than substantive content.

children.title: 1 Outcome Management Guide
1.1 Understanding Outcome Management
What Is Outcome Management?
children.summary: This section defines outcome management as a client-centric, collaborative approach to planning and service delivery that identifies real needs, sets goals, and tracks progress toward desired changes in behavior, knowledge, skills, condition, or status. It contrasts this with siloed, ineffective traditional practices. It explains why outcome management matters for better client results, reduced agency dependence and costs, and clearer organizational goals. It also describes support features such as assessments, multidisciplinary team collaboration tools, governance/sharing via an MDT portal, and defining client goals and objectives in an outcome plan.

children.title: Why Is Outcome Management Important?
children.summary: Outcome management is important because it helps agencies identify clients’ real needs, deliver appropriate services at the right time, and reduce long-term dependency and costs. It shifts agencies from siloed, problem-mismatched service delivery to a collaborative, client-centric approach focused on measurable outcomes such as self-sufficiency. The section also explains that clear outcome results can support funding, and that outcome management includes assessments, multidisciplinary team collaboration, goal setting, and progress monitoring within an outcome plan.

children.title: How Does Outcome Management Support This?
children.summary: Outcome Management supports a structured, client-centric process for improving client outcomes within an outcome plan. It enables configurable assessments to identify root causes of client needs, defines goals and objectives based on those needs, and tracks progress over time. It also supports multidisciplinary teamwork through collaboration tools like discussion boards, meeting scheduling, and minutes, plus controlled information sharing via the MDT Portal.

Outcome Management is a client-centric, collaborative service delivery approach focused on the results clients want, not just available services. This section explains how agencies use assessments, planning, and multidisciplinary teamwork to identify root causes of need, define goals and objectives, and track progress over time. It contrasts this with traditional siloed practices that can leave clients dependent longer and increase costs. It also notes organizational benefits such as clearer outcome targets and potential funding support.

children.title: 1.2 Configuring Outcome Management
Configuring Assessments
Configuring Assessment Types
children.summary: This section explains how Outcome Management lets agencies configure multiple assessment types through assessment definitions. It covers how each definition controls data collection and result calculation using IEG question scripts plus CDA or CER rule sets. It also notes settings for priority factors, reassessment, result display, and whether assessments apply to groups/households or to individuals, with implications for how questions are asked and results shown.

children.title: Configuring Assessment Roles
children.summary: This section explains how to set up assessment roles, factors, categories, and classifications. Roles define each client’s part in an assessment and must be assigned in the assessment definition and selected when running it. Factors represent needs or barriers to assess, and can be linked to planning objectives. Factors are grouped into categories such as need or barrier. Each category requires classifications that define possible result ranges, which can vary by factor even within the same classification.

children.title: Configuring Assessment Factors
children.summary: Explains how administrators configure assessment factors for a type of assessment. Factors represent client needs or barriers, can be included in planning, and examples include shelter, parenting skills, and substance abuse. Factors must be grouped into at least one category (such as need or barrier), and each category has classifications that define possible results for all factors in that category. Different factors can use different numeric ranges for the same classification.

children.title: Categorizing and Classifying Assessment Factors
children.summary: Explains how assessment factors are grouped into categories such as need or barrier, and how categories and classifications define possible results for assessments. Notes that each assessment must have at least one category, all factors must belong to a category, and all classifications in a category apply to its factors. Describes using classifications to set result ranges and gives examples where need and barrier factors have different classification labels and score ranges. Also notes some factors can support planning, not just result recording.

This section explains how Outcome Management assessments are configured. It covers defining assessment types (using IEG question scripts and CDA/CER rule sets), setting options such as reassessment, priority factors, result display, and whether assessments apply to groups/households or individuals. It also describes assessment roles, factors, categories, and classifications: roles identify client participation, factors capture needs or barriers, categories group factors, and classifications define result ranges that can differ by factor.

children.title: Configuring Outcome Plans
Configuring Outcome Plan Types
Associating Evidence
children.summary: This section explains how evidence types are associated with an outcome plan type. Administrators can configure one or more evidence types for a plan type; if configured, an Evidence tab appears when a client outcome plan is created, allowing those evidence items to be added. If no evidence type is configured, the Evidence tab is absent. This appears within the broader configuration of outcome plan types, alongside goals, objectives, factors, teams, visits, and printable content.

This section explains how agencies configure outcome plan types to fit different client needs. Administrators can define multiple plan types, each with its own allowed goals, objectives, and factors, so only relevant items appear when a plan is created. It also covers associating one or more evidence types with a plan type: if configured, an Evidence tab appears on the client’s outcome plan; if not, it does not.

children.title: Configuring Outcome Plan Goals
children.summary: Explains how outcome plan goals are configured in Outcome Management. Administrators can define goals once and share them across multiple outcome plan types, and case workers can later add those goals when creating a client’s plan. The surrounding page also describes outcome plan types and evidence associations, showing that each plan type can restrict which goals, objectives, and factors are available. Useful if you need how goals are set up and reused across plan types.

children.title: Configuring Outcome Plan Objectives
children.summary: This section explains how outcome plan objectives are configured. Administrators define reusable objectives that represent smaller steps toward an outcome plan’s goal, and case workers add them to individual plans. It also notes related configuration areas for outcome plan factors, multidisciplinary team collaboration, and visits, including when these features are enabled and how they support planning, assessment, shared access, and review.

children.title: Configuring Outcome Plan Factors
children.summary: This section explains how to configure outcome plan factors. It states that factors can be defined by an administrator and shared across outcome plan types, and unlike assessment factors they do not require a question script, CER rule set, or CDA Matrix. Only categories and classifications are needed so case workers can record results, such as outsourced GED assessments. It also notes that factors can optionally be marked for planning, allowing objectives and activities to be linked to them.

children.title: Configuring Multidisciplinary Teams
children.summary: This section explains configuration for multidisciplinary teams on outcome plans. Administrators can enable or restrict whether a team can be assigned to a specific outcome plan type and control what information team members can access, such as notes for certain roles like police officers. If enabled, assigning a multidisciplinary team supports collaboration between the case worker and outside disciplines through the Multidisciplinary Team Portal.

children.title: Configuring Visits
children.summary: This section explains how administrators configure whether visitation plans apply to a type of outcome plan. When enabled, caseworkers can create visitation plans and visitation logs on the outcome plan and evaluate visits during reviews. It notes that visitation plans are used to define and describe planned interactions between people, such as child welfare visits between a child in out-of-home placement and family members or other participants.

children.title: Configuring Printable Document Content
children.summary: This section explains how Outcome Management configures printable report content for outcome plans, including selectable items like goals, objectives, and assessments. It covers linking assessments to specific outcome plans/cases and controlling where assessments can be launched. It also introduces outcome management activities and the three activity types: services, referrals, and actions. Finally, it describes service configuration in CPM, including service delivery options, eligibility/authorization integration, payment handling, and key service offering settings.

This section explains how Outcome Management lets agencies configure outcome plans. It covers defining outcome plan types with their own allowed goals, objectives, factors, and optional evidence types; setting up reusable goals, objectives, and factors; and enabling multidisciplinary team collaboration and visit/visitation features for specific plan types. It also notes that factors can be planning-related, allowing objectives and activities to link to them, and that printable document content is another configurable aspect of outcome plans.

children.title: Associating Assessments with Outcome Plans and Cases
children.summary: Explains how assessments are linked to outcome plans and cases so workers can run them from specific plans or reviews; example: a Caregiver Strength and Needs Assessment may be limited to a Reunification plan and configured for use from the plan, a review, or both. Also introduces outcome management activities (services, referrals, actions) that caseworkers add to plans to address assessed needs, plus service configuration basics in CPM, including delivery processing modes and key service options.

children.title: Configuring Outcome Management Activities
Configuring Services
children.summary: This section explains how Outcome Management is configured for printable document content, assessment links, and activities, then focuses on services. It notes that services must be set up in the Provider Management (CPM) services registry before they can be added to an outcome plan. Services may be delivered directly or by third-party providers, and can use either product delivery processing or service delivery processing. It also lists key service options such as provider requirements, multi-client support, frequency, participation tracking, and provider rate settings.

children.title: Configuring Referrals
children.summary: This section explains how to configure outcome-plan items in Cúram: referrals, actions, evidence, and recommended objectives/activities. Referrals must be defined in the CPM services registry and can trigger notifications to clients and providers, with options like multi-client applicability and templates. Actions are defined in outcome management administration, with settings for cost, participation tracking, responsibility, and rename permission. It also covers associating evidence types with actions and setting up system-generated recommendations based on outcome plan type, assessment classifications, scores, and filtering conditions.

children.title: Configuring Actions
Evidence
children.summary: This section explains how to configure outcome plan items in Cúram 8.1.2, including referrals, actions, and evidence. It defines referrals as third-party services not paid by the agency, with notification and multi-client options. Actions are non-service activities that can include costs, participation tracking, responsibility, and rename settings. The evidence subsection says evidence can be attached to actions once an evidence type is linked, with options for read-only behavior, storage location, preview panel, and home page.

Explains how to configure outcome plan actions in Cúram 8.1.2. Actions must be defined in outcome management administration before use and can be non-service activities added to an outcome plan. Configuration controls multi-client support, cost, participation tracking, responsible party, and whether workers may rename the action. It also covers attaching evidence to actions once an evidence type is linked, including read-only settings, storage location (person or outcome plan), preview panel, and home page options.

This section introduces outcome management activities and related configuration for printable document content and assessments. It explains that caseworkers can generate outcome-plan reports from configurable content items, and assessments can be restricted to specific outcome plans and locations (plan, review, or both). It then defines activities as administrator-created items added to plans, with three types: services, referrals, and actions. Services must exist in the CPM services registry and can use product delivery or service delivery processing, with options for provider, multi-client, frequency, participation, and rate settings.

children.title: Configuring Recommended Objectives and Activities
children.summary: Explains how the system auto-recommends objectives and activities in outcome plans. Recommendations can be triggered by outcome plan type, factor classification, or assessment score/range, with examples like Job Search for Self Sufficiency and Alcohol Abuse Counseling for Substance Abuse. It also notes that conditions can filter recommendations using rule sets, assessment questions/answers, age, and gender. Useful if you need to configure when suggested objectives/activities appear for caseworkers.

This section explains how Outcome Management is configured for assessments, outcome plans, activities, and recommendations. It covers assessment types using IEG question scripts with CDA/CER rule sets, roles, factors, categories, and classifications, plus options like reassessment, group vs individual assessment, and result display. It also covers outcome plan types, goals, objectives, factors, evidence, multidisciplinary teams, visits, and printable content. Finally, it describes linking assessments to plans/cases, configuring services/referrals/actions, and auto-recommending objectives and activities based on plan type, scores, or conditions.

children.title: 1.3 Building an Outcome Plan to Achieve Positive Outcomes
Creating An Outcome Plan For Clients
children.summary: This section explains how Outcome Management supports creating client outcome plans. It covers recommendation generation and invalidation when configuration changes, plus the overall planning workflow: create a plan, identify and prioritize needs, assign the MDT, define goals and objectives, schedule activities, issue agreement, and develop a visitation plan. It also notes that plans can be created from an integrated case, outside a case, or automatically by the system.

children.title: Running An Assessment To Identify Client Needs
Creating An Assessment
children.summary: This section explains how to create an assessment in Cúram. Assessments can be created from an outcome plan, a review within a plan, or any case, and only members of that case/plan can be included. A predefined assessment is selected, then clients are assigned roles such as primary or secondary caregiver. The available roles and whether one or more clients can fill a role are controlled by application administration.

children.title: Completing Assessment Questionnaires
children.summary: Explains how assessment questionnaires are completed after selecting clients and roles. Uses predefined IEG question scripts to collect client information, with questions targeted to all clients, a subset, or a group depending on configuration. Notes help text access, completion via Next, and that answers feed either a CDA decision matrix or CER rules to generate results. Also covers how results are derived from answer scores and displayed automatically as a list or graph.

children.title: Deriving Assessment Results
children.summary: Explains how assessment results are derived from configured factors in the administration app. Answers from question scripts are mapped to scores in a CDA matrix or CER rule set to classify clients into outcomes such as Need or Strength (example: substance abuse scores from -5 to 3). Also notes that results are shown automatically after completing the assessment, either as a list or graph, depending on configuration.

children.title: Viewing Assessment Results
Using The Bar Chart View
children.summary: Explains how assessment results are viewed in the bar chart and radar chart displays, and how these formats help case workers interpret client needs. The bar chart uses bars per factor, with values below the x-axis indicating needs and the Y-axis configurable to show scores or classifications; a tab shows each factor’s result. Also covers prioritizing high-urgency factors and the use of guidance text, which can be configured by classification or specific score to help case workers respond appropriately.

children.title: Using The Radar Chart View
children.summary: Explains the radar chart view for assessment results in Cúram Outcome Management. Radar charts display each factor as a spoke with increments across the score range; points near the center indicate low scores and near the edge indicate high scores. The overall shape helps compare a client’s scores across factors and between clients. The section sits alongside bar chart/list result views and notes that the default graph type is set in assessment administration but can be switched by a case worker.

children.title: Prioritizing High Priority Needs
children.summary: Explains how assessment results are presented in list, bar chart, and radar chart views, including how needs and strengths are clustered and how charts help compare scores. Describes factor prioritization for highlighting urgent needs when agencies cannot address everything, using examples like housing. Also introduces guidance text, which is configured in administration and shown based on a client’s score or classification, with support for both range-based and specific-value guidance.

children.title: Using Guidance Text
children.summary: This section explains guidance text for assessment results: help text and resource links shown to case workers when a client receives a particular score or classification. Guidance appears only if configured in administration for a factor. It can be defined at the classification level (for example, Need for scores 0–5) or at a more specific score within that range. The section clarifies that displayed guidance depends on both the assessed result and the administration settings.

This section explains how assessment results are displayed after completion: as a list or graph, with client name, factor, classification, and score. It covers bar chart and radar chart views, including how bars/spokes represent scores, how needs can be clustered, and that the default graph type is set in administration but can be switched by a case worker. It also describes prioritizing urgent needs and configuring guidance text, which appears only when defined for a factor and can be based on a classification or specific score.

children.title: Identifying Additional Client Needs
children.summary: Explains how Outcome Management captures additional client needs beyond standard assessments, including separately defined factors like GED assessments and user-identified issues from caseworker interaction. It also describes the assessment approval workflow: administrators can require some assessments to be supervisor-approved, tasks are assigned for approval or rejection, and submission/approval metadata is recorded. Rejected assessments return to in progress and can be revised and resubmitted, with optional priority factor changes.

children.title: Approving or Rejecting an Assessment
children.summary: Explains the assessment approval workflow in Outcome Management. Administrators can configure a percentage of assessments to require supervisor approval. Assessments are auto-approved for users with approval privileges; others are assigned a task with links to approve or reject. The system records submitter, submission date, approver/rejector, and decision date. Statuses include submitted, complete for approved, and in progress for rejected assessments, which must be resumed and resubmitted. Priority factors can be changed before resubmission if applicable.

children.title: Completing An Assessment
children.summary: Assessment completion occurs once approved, either automatically by the system or manually by a case supervisor if manual approval is required. The section also explains assigning multidisciplinary teams to outcome plans: either predefined teams reused across plans or individual teams created for a specific family, with configuration set in administration. It covers collaboration features for sharing selected case information with team members via the Multidisciplinary Team Portal, including role-based sharing settings and member-level overrides.

Explains how Outcome Management assessments identify client needs: creating an assessment from a case or outcome plan, selecting predefined assessment types, assigning clients and roles, and completing IEG-based questionnaires. It covers result derivation via CDA matrices or CER rules, automatic display as list or graph (bar or radar), prioritizing urgent needs, and guidance text. Also notes additional nonstandard client needs can be recorded, and assessments may require supervisor approval, with submitted/approved/rejected status handling and resubmission after rejection.

children.title: Assigning A Multidisciplinary Team To An Outcome Plan
Collaborating With The Multidisciplinary Team
Tools That Support Collaboration
children.summary: This section describes collaboration tools within an outcome plan, focused on discussion boards for multidisciplinary teams. It explains that team members can share advice and important information, and boards can be configured to email members when discussions or comments are posted. It also notes that discussions are only available when a multidisciplinary team is assigned to the outcome plan, and that caseworkers access these functions from the outcome plan while team members use the Multidisciplinary Team Portal.

Explains how Outcome Management supports collaboration with multidisciplinary teams in an outcome plan. It covers sharing notes, attachments, and activities with selected team members based on role- and individual-level configuration, with optional email notifications. It also describes discussion boards for team advice and information sharing, including configurable email alerts for posts and comments. Discussions are available only when a multidisciplinary team is assigned. Caseworkers use these tools from the outcome plan, while team members access them through the Multidisciplinary Team Portal.

Describes how an outcome plan can be assigned either a predefined multidisciplinary team or a family-specific team, configured via outcome plan administration. Explains adding members from existing teams, registering new members, or adding agency users. Covers collaboration features: sharing notes, attachments, and activities with team members based on role- and individual-level permissions, with optional email notifications. Also includes discussion boards for advice and information sharing, configurable email alerts, and notes that discussions are only available when a multidisciplinary team is assigned.

children.title: Establishing The Goals And Objectives Of The Outcome Plan
Adding Goals To The Outcome Plan
children.summary: This section explains how outcome plan goals and objectives are defined and added. It distinguishes a plan’s main goal from smaller client objectives, emphasizing collaboration with the client. It covers adding goals to the plan or to individual clients, with configuration controlling which is allowed. A goal requires a reason and expected achievement date, which can be changed with history tracked. Multiple goals may exist over time, but only one can be active at once. Objectives can also be optionally added for one or more clients.

children.title: Adding Objectives To The Outcome Plan
Associating Factors To These Objectives
children.summary: Explains how objectives are added to an outcome plan, including dragging from recommendations or selecting from the objectives list, and notes required details like reason and expected achievement date (with editable history). Introduces associating client-related factors to objectives to track activities supporting them, and transitions into planning activities to address client needs. Also briefly describes the outcome plan workspace, where objectives and activities can be viewed, linked, favored, and tracked on a daily, monthly, or quarterly timeline.

Explains that outcome plans can optionally include objectives for one or more clients, selected from objectives configured in outcome plan administration. Objectives are smaller steps toward the plan goal and are meant to be set collaboratively with the client to support motivation and progress. This section is a brief setup point for adding objectives before further details on linking activities and tracking support.

Explains how outcome plan goals and objectives are defined and added. The main goal is the primary desired outcome, while objectives are smaller client steps toward that goal, both set collaboratively with the client to support motivation and progress. Goals may be added at the plan level or for individual clients depending on configuration, require a reason and expected achievement date, and date changes are history-tracked. Multiple goals can exist over time, but only one can be active at once. Objectives are optional and selected from configured options.

children.title: Planning Activities To Address Client Needs
The Workspace
The Objective workspace
children.summary: The objective workspace lets caseworkers quickly access recommended and recently added objectives and activities, and schedule new ones in an outcome plan. It provides a central view of all client objectives and activities, showing activity hierarchy and allowing activities to be linked to objectives or added as stand-alone items. The left workspace view lists scheduled items, with associated activities nested under objectives and favorites supported. A timeline view offers daily, monthly, and quarterly perspectives.

children.title: (deprecated) The Activity Workspace
children.summary: Describes the deprecated Activity Workspace in Cúram Outcome Management. It provides a central view of current client activities and supports finding and scheduling activities via recommendations, recently added items, and a timeline. The workspace has three areas: a left timeline with daily/weekly/monthly and analysis views; a right recommendations panel with Plan and Factor tabs plus an accessible listing; and a bottom panel showing details for selected clients, factors, recommended or recently added activities, and timeline items.

The workspace in an outcome plan lets caseworkers access recommended and recently added objectives and activities and quickly schedule new ones. The objective workspace provides a central view of all scheduled objectives and activities, supports linking activities to objectives or adding them as stand-alone items, and shows their hierarchy. The left workspace view lists scheduled items, including favorites, with activities nested under objectives. A timeline view offers daily, monthly, and quarterly perspectives.

children.title: Tools For Matching Activities To Client Needs
Recommending Activities For Assessment Results And Outcome Plan Types
children.summary: This section explains how assessment results and outcome plan types generate activity recommendations for case workers. It covers recommendations based on activities added by other workers, configurable “recently added” activity lists, and guidance text tied to assessment classifications or scores. It also describes two ways to add activities to an outcome plan: from the recommendations panel/listing or by searching the activities list. Finally, it notes common fields required for action, service, and referral activities, especially client selection and reason.

children.title: Activities Added By Other Case Workers
children.summary: This section explains how activities added by other case workers appear as recommendations in the activity workspace, with admin-configurable limits for how many recent activities and days are considered. It also describes guidance text as a recommendation aid based on assessment factors and scores. Finally, it outlines two ways to add activities to an outcome plan—via recommended activities or direct search—and notes common required fields like selecting client(s) and specifying a reason.

children.title: Activity Recommendations In Guidance Text
children.summary: This section explains how activity recommendations appear in guidance text and how they help case workers act consistently. It notes that guidance can be configured for assessment factors inside or outside assessments and shown when a client reaches a specific classification or score. It also covers adding activities to an outcome plan either from recommendations or by searching the activity list, and lists common required activity fields such as client selection and reason.

This section introduces Outcome Management tools for matching activities to client needs. It explains the activity workspace: a timeline on the left, recommendations on the right, and details in the bottom panel. Recommendations include plan-based and factor-based activities, recently added activities, and accessible listings. Case workers can filter by clients, outcomes, priority factors, and factors, view daily/weekly/monthly/analysis views, and drag recommended items into the workspace. Selecting items shows reasons, added-by details, dates, guidance, and client progress.

children.title: Adding Activities To The Outcome Plan
children.summary: This section explains how activities are added to an outcome plan and what common fields must be provided. It says activities can be added either by dragging a recommended activity from the plan workspace/recommendations list or by creating a new activity from the activities list. Recommended activities may come from assessment guidance or from activities recently added by other case workers, with configurable display limits. It also notes key creation requirements: at least one client must be selected, and a reason for the activity must be entered.

children.title: Scheduling A Service For A Client
children.summary: Explains how a worker schedules a client service by first selecting a service via taxonomy search, name search, or system recommendation from the CPM services registry. The chosen service determines what details can be entered, which may include number of units, authorized rate, frequency, required participation, nominee payment, owner, and provider. Also notes related scheduling context on this page: activity sensitivity security, checking client availability against existing activities, selecting factors, and sharing activities with multidisciplinary team members.

children.title: Referring A Client For A Service
children.summary: Explains how to refer a client for a service: a case worker selects a service from the CPM services registry or a system recommendation, and then specifies referral details such as date, follow-up, and notifications to client/provider. Also introduces related action scheduling, including action selection, statuses (Not Started/In Progress), optional renaming, cost configuration (no/fixed/user-specified), required participation frequency/duration, and assigning an owner.

children.title: Scheduling An Action For A Client
Cost
children.summary: Explains action cost configuration during scheduling in Outcome Management. Actions may have no cost, a fixed cost, or a user-specified/estimated cost depending on administrator settings. If fixed, the actual cost is shown at creation; if user-specified, the case worker enters an estimated cost; if no cost, it is hidden. Also briefly introduces required participation (frequency/duration) and owner assignment options for actions.

children.title: Required Participation
children.summary: Explains “required participation” for actions in Outcome Management: some actions can be configured to require a client to meet a specified frequency and duration of participation during action creation. This supports cases such as sanctions on benefits if participation requirements are not met. Useful for understanding when and how case workers must enter participation details for an action.

children.title: Assigning an Owner
children.summary: Explains how an action owner is assigned in outcome management. The owner is the person responsible for completing the action and can be the creating case worker, an MDT member, the client, or another system user. Assignment behavior is controlled by configuration settings, including the option to automatically default the owner to the worker creating the action, eliminating the need for manual selection.

children.title: Assigning Responsibility
children.summary: Explains how action responsibility is assigned in Cúram Outcome Management: responsibility can go to a client, a user, or an MDT member, with configuration rules that may default it automatically (for example, to the creating worker). Also introduces client agreements on outcome plans: caseworkers create agreements for one or more clients and signatories, select covered activities, and may need supervisor approval before issuance, depending on configured approval criteria.

Explains how to schedule an action for a client in Outcome Management. A case worker selects an action from configured or system-recommended options, which determines what details can be entered. Newly created actions are Not Started if the start date is future, otherwise In Progress. Covers action cost settings (no cost, fixed, or user-specified estimate), required participation (frequency/duration), and owner assignment rules, including automatic defaulting to the creating worker.

This section explains how case workers plan activities to meet client needs in Outcome Management. It covers the objective and activity workspaces, including scheduled items, recommendations, favorites, hierarchy, and timeline views. It also describes how recommended or new activities are added to an outcome plan, common creation fields like client, reason, sensitivity, dates, factors, and collaboration, and how workers schedule services, referrals, and actions, including selection methods and key configuration options such as cost, required participation, owner, and status.

children.title: Managing Client Agreements
Creating Client Agreements
children.summary: This section explains how client agreements are created in Outcome Management. A caseworker selects one or more clients and signatories, then chooses the activities covered by the agreement before completing it for issue. It also notes that agreements may require supervisor approval based on configured approval criteria. If approval is needed, a task is assigned to a user with approval privileges, who can review, approve, or reject the agreement via links to the outcome plan.

children.title: Approving Client Agreements
children.summary: Explains the client agreement approval workflow in Outcome Management. After a case worker marks an agreement complete and ready to issue, it may require supervisor approval based on administrator-defined approval criteria (for example, a percentage of agreements). Agreements created by users with approval privileges are auto-approved; others are submitted for manual approval. An approval task is assigned to an authorized user and includes links to the outcome plan and to approve or reject the agreement.

children.title: Issuing Client Agreements
children.summary: Explains issuing approved client agreements: once completed and approved, an agreement can be sent to a signatory/client by email or post based on configuration. Reissues are allowed, and each issuance/reissuance is logged with date, issuer, and recipient. Also covers approval/rejection tracking: submission and decision details are recorded; approved agreements become Completed, rejected ones remain Open for rework. Ends with a brief note on recording signed dates and storing scanned signed agreements as attachments.

children.title: Signing Client Agreements
children.summary: This section explains what happens when a client agreement is signed. After a case worker receives the signed agreement, they can record the signed date and scan the document as an attachment. It appears alongside related agreement workflow details such as approval/rejection tracking, issuing and reissuing agreements, and agreement status changes.

Explains how Outcome Management client agreements are created and approved. Caseworkers create one or more agreements on an outcome plan by selecting clients, signatories, and covered activities, then mark the agreement complete for issue. Administrators can configure approval criteria, such as a percentage requiring supervisor review. Agreements created by users with approval privileges are auto-approved; others generate an approval task with links to the outcome plan and approve/reject actions.

children.title: Developing Visitation Plans
Tracking Actual Visits
children.summary: Explains how visitation logs record actual visits for a visitation plan. Caseworkers enter the clients visited, visitors, visit date, interaction quality, contact method, and whether the visit was scheduled, unscheduled, or rescheduled; rescheduled visits require a reason. A narrative description of the visit is also required. Useful for understanding how to document and track real interactions after a visitation plan is created.

This section explains how to create visitation plans that define planned interactions between clients and visitors, including who participates, when, where, how often, and for how long visits occur. It notes these plans are important for child welfare/out-of-home placements and may be court ordered or mutually agreed. It also describes configuration-based age limits that filter eligible clients for visitation plans. The section transitions into tracking actual visits via visitation logs, which record visit details and required narrative descriptions.

This section explains the full Outcome Management workflow for building a plan to achieve client goals: creating plans from an integrated case, outside a case, or automatically; identifying needs via assessments; assigning an MDT; defining goals/objectives; scheduling activities/services/referrals/actions; issuing and approving client agreements; and developing visitation plans, especially for child welfare/out-of-home placements. It also notes recommendation generation and invalidation when administration settings change, plus tracking actual visits with visitation logs.

children.title: 1.4 Monitoring Client Progress
Outcome Plan Analysis View
children.summary: This section explains the Outcome Plan Analysis View, a visual tool for monitoring client progress in Cúram Outcome Management. It shows the outcome plan, clients, objectives, factors, activities, and their relationships as connected nodes, with client/objective/factor nodes expandable or collapsible. The section also introduces monitoring features such as reassessments, tracking reviews and progress against plan elements, and generating reports for meetings or court use. It includes how reassessments work for groups versus individual clients.

children.title: Reassessing Clients Needs Over Time
Running A Reassessment
children.summary: Explains how reassessment supports monitoring client progress over time by re-running an assessment to identify current needs and measure change. Notes that agencies can reassess a client or group of clients, with group reassessments limited to clients in the original assessment group, while individual reassessments may include clients not previously assessed. Useful for tracking progress toward goals such as self-sufficiency on priority factors.

children.title: Comparing Assessment Results
children.summary: This section explains how to compare an initial assessment with a reassessment, or two reassessments, to identify changes in client progress. It covers selecting which assessments to compare, viewing factor classifications and scores for each client, and reviewing the recorded answers to see what caused differences. It also notes options to highlight or clear differences and reset the comparison.

children.title: Viewing Assessment Results History
children.summary: This section explains assessment history access: caseworkers can review the results achieved each time a client or client group was assessed. It notes that history records include which client(s) were assessed and the role(s) they played. The surrounding text also covers reassessment options (reassessing all or selected factors, updating priority factors, and changing client roles if enabled) and comparing assessment results between the initial assessment and reassessments, including scores, classifications, answers, and highlighted differences.

Explains how Outcome Management supports monitoring client progress by reassessing clients over time, either individually or as a group. Covers rerunning assessments on all or selected factors, updating priority factors from reassessment results, and optionally changing client roles during reassessment. Also describes comparing an initial assessment with a reassessment or two reassessments to review scores, classifications, answers, and highlighted differences, plus viewing assessment history to see what results were achieved each time and which clients/roles were assessed.

children.title: Ongoing Assessment Management
Viewing Assessment Summary Information
children.summary: Explains how case workers view assessment summary information after completion. The summary shows who was assessed, each client’s role, start and completion times, and results in either a list or graph. If graphical display is enabled, users can switch between bar and radar graphs, with tabs showing line graphs of each factor over time. This section also introduces related assessment-management functions, including reviewing recorded answers, viewing approval requests, and generating assessment reports.

children.title: Reviewing Answers To Assessment Questions
children.summary: Explains how case workers review assessment responses after completion. The section notes that assessments let users view the answers recorded for clients, alongside related ongoing assessment management features such as viewing assessment summary information, approval request history, and generating assessment reports. It also references graphical result displays (bar or radar graphs, with per-factor line graphs) used in the assessment summary context.

children.title: Viewing Assessment Approval Requests
children.summary: Shows the list of assessment approval requests sent to a case supervisor for approval. Each request includes the requester’s name, submission date, approver or rejecter, decision date, and decision status. If no decision has been made, the request is marked Submitted. Useful for reviewing assessment approval history and current approval outcomes.

children.title: Assessment Report
children.summary: Explains the Assessment Report in Outcome Management, used to support reviews and meetings about client assessments. The report includes an overview of all assessed clients with tabular results for all factors and assessment notes, plus a separate section for each client showing their latest assessment results, the latest questions and answers, and all questions and answers from reassessments.

children.title: Closing An Assessment
children.summary: Explains how an assessment can be closed, including that it may be closed at any time and cannot be reopened once closed. Describes assessment statuses—in-progress, submitted, complete, canceled, closed—and what each means and when transitions are allowed. Also covers factor reprioritization in outcome plans, noting that case workers can reorder factors over time and the system keeps history. Finally, introduces lapsed factors, which stop producing recommendations when reassessment is overdue.

children.title: Assessment Statuses
children.summary: Explains assessment lifecycle statuses and related processing rules. The five statuses are in-progress, submitted, complete, canceled, and closed, with definitions and allowed transitions: only in-progress assessments can be submitted or canceled; complete assessments can be closed; closed assessments cannot be reopened. Also briefly introduces closing assessments, factor reprioritization in outcome plans, and lapsed factors, where outdated factors stop producing recommendations.

Covers post-completion assessment management in Outcome Management. Case workers can view assessment summaries showing assessed clients, roles, start/completion times, and results as lists or graphs; if enabled, they can switch between bar and radar graphs and view factor trend line graphs. It also covers reviewing recorded answers, checking assessment approval request history and statuses, and generating assessment reports with overview and per-client sections including latest results, questions/answers, reassessment history, and notes.

children.title: Reprioritizing Factors
children.summary: Explains factor reprioritization in Outcome Management: case workers can reorder factors in an outcome plan to address the most urgent client needs first, such as prioritizing a low Parenting score. The system supports changing priorities over time and keeps a history of those changes. The surrounding text also introduces assessment closure/statuses and notes that lapsed factors are not reassessed for a long period, which stops recommendations from appearing on the activity workspace.

children.title: Lapsing Factors
children.summary: Explains lapsing factors in Outcome Management. A factor becomes lapsed when it has not been reassessed for a long period and its next assessment due date has passed. When a factor is lapsed, the system does not produce recommendations for that factor on the activity workspace.

children.title: Managing Outcome Plan Reviews
Creating A Review
children.summary: Explains how outcome plan reviews are created and used to track changes made during the review period. Covers review scheduling via first-review timing and review frequency, next review date display, and creation of a review with period, expected completion date, type, narrative, and notes. Notes that only one review can be open at a time, and any added, reviewed, or completed goals, objectives, or activities are automatically linked to the open review for traceability.

children.title: Reviewing Activities, Goals And Objectives
children.summary: Explains how outcome plan reviews track changes to activities, goals, and objectives. When a review is open, any addition, review, or completion of these elements is automatically linked to that review, so case workers can see what changed and why. Covers creating reviews, expected completion dates, review frequency/next review date, notes and narrative, and that only one review can be open at a time. Includes an example showing a service added after a review is associated with that review.

children.title: Review Assessments
children.summary: Explains how reviews track added, completed, reviewed, and unreviewed plan elements, with action history for activities, goals, and objectives; referrals are excluded from review tracking. Covers integrated Review Assessments, which can be run during a review if enabled by configuration. Also describes evaluating visit effectiveness/compliance during review periods, including criteria for compliant visits and compliance percentage calculation. Ends by noting review statuses: Open, Completed, and Cancelled.

children.title: Evaluating Visits
children.summary: Explains how outcome management evaluates visits during plan reviews. For each visited client/visitor with a visitation plan in the review period, a visit review is created and displays visits that occurred plus compliance. Compliance depends on matching contact method, being scheduled or properly rescheduled, and meeting any minimum duration; unscheduled visits are noncompliant. Compliance percentage is calculated as (actual compliant visits/planned visits) × 100 and mapped to configured ranges. Also introduces review statuses: Open, Completed, and Cancelled.

children.title: Review Statuses
children.summary: Explains review status handling in outcome management. Reviews can be Open, Completed, or Cancelled, with status changing during review processing. The section sits after discussion of review element tracking (added, completed, reviewed items), review assessments, and visit evaluation/compliance. Useful for understanding how review progress is represented and managed, especially when deciding the lifecycle state of a review.

Explains how outcome plan reviews are created, scheduled, and used to track plan changes. Covers first-review timing, review frequency, next review date, and review details such as period, expected completion date, type, narrative, and notes. While a review is open, added, reviewed, or completed goals, objectives, and activities are automatically linked to it; only one review can be open at a time. Also introduces review assessments, visit evaluation/compliance during the review period, and the review statuses Open, Completed, and Cancelled.

children.title: Recording Client Participation
Recording Daily Participation
children.summary: Explains how case workers record client participation within an outcome plan. Covers daily participation for actions/services, including hours attended or absent on a specific day, handling mismatches with scheduled participation, and automatic updating of service/action dates when participation falls outside the original period. Also describes scheduled attendance, where the system creates participation records for past or projected periods based on the original schedule, and allows later correction of individual records.

children.title: Recording Scheduled Attendance
children.summary: Explains how case workers record scheduled attendance within an outcome plan. Scheduled attendance can cover past or future periods (projected attendance) when the client is expected to attend. The system auto-creates participation records based on the original action/service schedule, such as weekly Monday attendance for a date range. If actual attendance differs or the client was absent on projected dates, the individual records can be updated afterward.

children.title: Recording Scheduled Absence
children.summary: Explains how Outcome Management handles scheduled absence, allowing past or projected absences to auto-create absence records based on service participation, replacing any prior attendance and later editable if needed. Also covers recording client progress with configurable values and notes, graphical progress tracking, and where progress is allowed by service settings. Finally, introduces activity cost tracking via a cost statement showing estimated and actual costs for actions and services, with costs derived from caseworker-entered or fixed/service payment data.

Explains how case workers record client participation in outcome plans. Covers daily participation for actions/services, including hours attended or absent on a specific day, warnings when entered participation differs from the original schedule, and automatic extension of service/action dates if participation falls outside the planned period. Also covers scheduled attendance, where past or projected attendance records are auto-created from the original schedule (for example, weekly Monday attendance) and can be corrected later if actual attendance or absence differs.

children.title: Evaluating Client Progress
children.summary: Explains how Outcome Management lets case workers record client progress between formal assessments by choosing configurable progress values and adding notes, with graphical progress tracking. Progress can be recorded for goals, objectives, factors, services, and actions, but service-level progress is configurable and may be disabled for some services. Also notes the adjacent topic of tracking activity cost, including estimated vs. actual cost statements for actions and services.

children.title: Tracking Activity Cost
children.summary: Explains activity cost tracking in Outcome Management. A cost statement shows estimated and actual costs for actions and services on a plan, helping identify rising costs and compare provider costs. Estimated action cost comes from a case-worker-entered estimate or a fixed admin-configured cost; estimated service cost is calculated on service creation. Actual action cost comes from the completion cost or fixed admin cost; actual service cost is based on payments made.

children.title: Additional Tools For Assisting Caseworkers
Case Worker Summary Views
Next Reviews
children.summary: Describes the “Next Reviews” summary in Outcome Management. It lists outcome plans owned by the current case worker with reviews due within a configurable number of days or already overdue. Default settings show reviews due in 1 day and include overdue items, which are highlighted by days overdue. This section sits within case worker summary views alongside “Ongoing Reviews” and productivity pages.

children.title: Ongoing Reviews
children.summary: Describes Outcome Management caseworker summary views and productivity pages for highlighting urgent work. It introduces Next Reviews, which lists outcome plans with reviews due soon or overdue, configurable by days and overdue inclusion, and highlights overdue days. Ongoing Reviews lists reviews currently in progress for outcome plans owned by the logged-in user. Also notes additional productivity pages such as Items Due To Complete, with filters that persist between sessions.

children.title: Items Due To Complete
children.summary: This section describes Outcome Management productivity pages for caseworkers, focusing on identifying work needing attention. It explains how the pages use filters and persisted settings, and lists the available pages: Items Due To Complete, Activities Due To Start, and Items Past Expected End Date. It also covers related summary views such as Next Reviews and Ongoing Reviews. The excerpt specifically starts the Items Due To Complete page, which lists plans, goals, objectives, services, actions, referrals, and reviews due to complete within a specified timeframe.

children.title: Activities Due To Start
children.summary: Describes the Activities Due To Start productivity page, which lists activities due to start within a user-set number of days, inclusive of today. Results are limited to activities on plans where the case worker is the plan owner or an MDT member. This page mirrors other Outcome Management productivity views and sits among related sections on items past expected end date, my outcome plans, attachments, and outcome plan reports. Useful if you need filtering and display rules for upcoming activities.

children.title: Items Past Expected End Date
children.summary: This section describes the “Items Past Expected End Date” productivity page in Outcome Management. It lists plans, goals, objectives, services, actions, and reviews that are overdue by a user-specified number of days and not yet marked complete. Results are limited to items on plans where the case worker is the plan owner or an MDT member. Users can filter by item type and minimum days past the expected end date; for example, 3 returns items overdue by 3+ days.

Outcome Management’s Case Worker Summary Views provide quick-access summaries and productivity pages for urgent work. It includes Next Reviews, showing outcome plans owned by the logged-in case worker with reviews due soon or overdue, configurable by days and overdue inclusion; Ongoing Reviews, listing reviews currently in progress; and productivity pages such as Items Due To Complete, Activities Due To Start, and Items Past Expected End Date. Filters are required initially, then persisted between sessions.

children.title: My Outcome Plans View
children.summary: This section explains the My Outcome Plans view in Outcome Management. It shows how case workers can see open outcome plans they are involved in as owner or MDT member, alongside related productivity views for items due to start, due to complete, or past expected end dates. It also covers attaching supplemental files to plans and associated items, and generating outcome plan reports, including overall and item-level versions with selectable content based on administration settings.

children.title: Attaching Files
children.summary: This section explains file attachments in Outcome Management. It states that attachments work like case attachments, allowing supplemental files to be added to outcome plans and to services, referrals, actions, and agreements. Attached files can be accessed by other users who have the appropriate security privileges.

children.title: Outcome Plan Report
children.summary: This section describes the Outcome Plan Report feature for case workers. It explains that two report versions are available: an overall outcome plan report and a short report for individual items. The overall report can include selected content items such as goals, objectives, activities, and factors, based on admin-configured options. If no items are selected, the report includes a table of contents and an overview with outcome plan details and clients on the plan.

children.title: Managing Correspondence
children.summary: Explains how workers manage correspondence in Cúram, including sending/printing communications by paper, phone, email, or integrated templates/servers. Also covers sending referral letters to clients/providers. The section then outlines note handling for outcome plans and assessments: adding free-text notes, priority/sensitivity ratings, limited editability, note history, and configuration references. It also briefly introduces case relationships between outcome plans and integrated cases, including manual and automatic creation.

children.title: Recording and Updating Notes
children.summary: This section explains how users add and manage notes on outcome plan elements and assessments. Notes are free text, can be prioritized and sensitivity-rated, and may be restricted by user access. Outcome plan and assessment notes can be modified to update priority, sensitivity, and append text; original text may be editable for a limited configured period. Notes on services, actions, factors, objectives, and goals cannot be modified. The system keeps full note history with versions, timestamps, and user details.

children.title: Related Cases
children.summary: Explains case relationships between an outcome plan and an integrated case. These links can be created manually by a worker or automatically during case processing, such as when an outcome plan is created from an integrated case. The section appears in the broader context of outcome plan management, including correspondence, notes, and completing the plan. Useful if you need how related cases are linked and when relationship records are created.

Outcome Management adds caseworker tools for prioritizing urgent work: summary views for Next Reviews and Ongoing Reviews, plus productivity pages for items due to complete, activities due to start, and items past expected end date. Filters are set on first use and persist. It also includes My Outcome Plans for open plans the worker owns or joins as MDT member, file attachments on plans and related items, and outcome plan reports with overall and item-level versions whose contents depend on admin configuration.

children.title: Completing The Outcome Plan
Completing Actions And Services
children.summary: Explains how to complete actions/services and record outcomes in an outcome plan. Actions or services are marked complete when the client no longer participates, with outcomes of successful or not successful; unsuccessful completions require a reason, and actions may also record actual cost if configured. Also covers completing goals and objectives, where outcomes show whether the client attained them and require a reason if not attained. Advises closing the outcome plan only after all goals and objectives have outcomes, changing status from Open to Closed.

children.title: Completing And Recording Outcomes For Goals And Objectives
children.summary: Explains how to complete and record outcomes for goals, objectives, actions, and services in an outcome plan. Actions/services are marked completed when participation ends, with outcomes recorded as successful or not successful and a reason required if unsuccessful; actual cost may also be entered if configured. Goals/objectives are completed similarly, indicating whether the client achieved the intended outcome and requiring a reason if not attained. Advises closing the outcome plan only after all goals and objectives have outcomes recorded, changing status from Open to Closed.

children.title: Closing The Outcome Plan
children.summary: Explains how to complete actions, services, goals, and objectives in an outcome plan by recording whether each was successful or not, with a required reason when unsuccessful and optional actual cost for actions. Emphasizes that these completion records help assess client progress and future service use. Also states that an outcome plan can be closed at any time, but should ideally be closed only after all goals and objectives have an outcome recorded; closing changes status from Open to Closed.

Explains how to complete an outcome plan by reviewing each element and recording outcomes for actions, services, goals, and objectives. Actions/services are completed when participation ends, with successful or not successful outcomes; unsuccessful completions require a reason, and actual cost may be entered for configured actions. Goals/objectives are marked attained or not attained, with a reason required if not attained. The plan can be closed at any time, but should ideally be closed only after all goals and objectives have outcomes recorded; status changes from Open to Closed.

Explains how caseworkers monitor client progress in Outcome Management using an analysis view, reassessments, assessment history/comparison, ongoing assessment summaries, reports, factor reprioritization, lapsed factors, and progress tracking. The analysis view shows outcome plans, clients, objectives, factors, and activities as expandable connected nodes. Reassessments can be run for individuals or groups, with optional factor subset and role changes; results can be compared and viewed over time. Also covers assessment closure/statuses, review planning, participation recording, activity cost tracking, and tools for prioritizing urgent work.

Outcome Management Guide explains a client-centric, collaborative approach to planning services around desired client outcomes rather than available services. It covers using assessments to identify root causes and needs, then building outcome plans with goals, objectives, activities, multidisciplinary teams, reassessments, reviews, and progress tracking. It also documents configuration of assessment types, roles, factors, classifications, and plan/recommendation behavior, plus reporting, notes, attachments, correspondence, and plan completion/closure. Useful for understanding both the business model and the main configuration/runtime features of Outcome Management.
